input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin system under study is infrastructure assets within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, with buildings as a primary example. Key characteristics include the integration of static models with runtime data across the entire building life cycle (from initial design through construction to operation and refurbishment), enabling data-driven management, real-time insights, predictive analytics, and optimization for reduced operational costs and enhanced stakeholder collaboration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real-time monitoring (via continuous data integration), predictive analytics for infrastructure health and performance forecasting, and optimization of operational processes. These services support stakeholders in the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, enabling data-driven decision-making throughout the building life cycle from initial design to refurbishments and operations.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, enabling immediate monitoring and control operations.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Centralized architecture with a DT Framework acting as a hub coordinating multiple digital twin instances; Building Control Engineers handle device modeling and deployment, Facility Managers perform real-time monitoring and control, and DT Engineers integrate custom services, each with defined responsibilities within holistic building lifecycle management.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Modular architecture with standardized extension points for service integration and runtime configuration management, where the DT Framework orchestrates bidirectional data exchange across roles to enable holistic building lifecycle management.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Nonfunctional requirements (NF_X) address critical aspects of interoperability across heterogeneous systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The system utilizes BIM-based geometric models enriched with semantic data for Building Control System (BCS) components (sensors, actuators, controllers). Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) transforms these into structured digital twin artifacts. Real-time data exchange synchronizes physical and digital states, while interactive visualization supports operational monitoring and control.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto middleware to automate the extraction of domain semantics and generation of runtime interfaces from Building Information Modeling (BIM) models, facilitating real-time data synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The DT Framework enables bidirectional communication to transmit control commands from Facility Managers through the digital twin interface to Building Control Systems for real-time monitoring and control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT Framework utilizes Eclipse Hono and Ditto middleware for technical connectivity, extracting tenant and device identifiers or endpoint addresses as metadata to support dynamic registration of physical assets.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle comprises initial device modeling within BIM environments by Building Control Engineers, followed by Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) based extraction of structured representations and automatic generation of deployable artifacts. Operational phases include real-time data synchronization, interactive visualization for monitoring and control, and evolution through modular extension points enabling integration of custom services by DT Engineers to address changing requirements.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process utilizes Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention. Evolution is managed via a modular architecture with standardized extension points for integrating custom services and analytics, ensuring adaptability to operational changes.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Real-time data exchange ensures continuous synchronization between physical and digital twins, with interactive visualization providing immersive interfaces for Facility Managers to monitor system states and operate infrastructure. Custom analytics services integrated via standardized extension points generate actionable insights, supporting data-driven decision-making processes with confidence derived from validated real-time data streams.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework adheres to Building Information Modeling (BIM) standards and AECO-specific system requirements, utilizing standardized extension points to ensure interoperability with existing protocols and heterogeneous environments, thereby enabling consistent data exchange and compliance with industry specifications.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly accurate and specific, correctly identifying the AECO domain, buildings as the primary physical asset, and the key mechanism of integrating static models with runtime data. It also correctly lists the benefits and lifecycle scope.

The extraction effectively captures the specific services provided, such as real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, and optimization. It correctly identifies the target stakeholders (AECO) and the context of the building lifecycle for data-driven decision-making.

The source text explicitly lists specific tools and frameworks used in the Digital Twin implementation, such as Eclipse Hono, Ditto, and Unreal Engine, but the extraction states 'Not in Document'. Supported by quotes: 'The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange' and 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the temporal aspects described in the text, specifically the focus on real-time and continuous synchronization. Supported by quote: 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts'.

The extraction correctly identifies the centralized hub architecture and the specific responsibilities of the three roles defined in the text. Supported by quotes: 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub', 'Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices', 'Facility Manager Monitors system states', and 'DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services'.

The extraction accurately synthesizes the modular architecture, extension points, and orchestration capabilities described in the Abstract and Section III. Supported by quotes: 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms', 'facilitating bidirectional data exchange', and 'Runtime configuration management'.

The extracted description is a verbatim match for the source text in Section III.A, which states that 'nonfunctional requirements (NF_X) address critical aspects of interoperability across heterogeneous systems'.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction provides a highly accurate and detailed summary of the system's models and data workflow as described in the source. It correctly identifies the use of BIM as a semantic backbone, the role of Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) in transforming models, the use of Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange, and Unreal Engine for visualization. This aligns perfectly with the abstract and introduction.

The source text discusses a framework for modeling and configuring actuators (e.g., 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment'), but it does not describe the physical actuators themselves, their specific types, actuation ranges, or safety constraints as required by the characteristic definition. Therefore, the assessment that this information is 'Not in Document' is correct.

While the text mentions sensors in the context of modeling and data integration ('post-occupancy sensor data', 'define sensors...'), it does not provide specific technical details about the sensing infrastructure such as sensor types, sampling frequencies, or accuracy specifications. The document focuses on the framework rather than the hardware specifications, so 'Not in Document' is appropriate.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source documents discuss validation and quality assurance aspects. The text mentions quality assurance in the context of MDE, synchronization techniques to handle semantic drift, and a specific metric (ODTE) for evaluation. Supported by quotes: 'promoting compliance, reuse, and quality assurance', 'semantic drift... necessity of synchronization techniques', and 'evaluate the entanglement... using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric'.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction provides a comprehensive and accurate summary of the Digital Twin lifecycle as described in the text, covering initial modeling, deployment, operation, and evolution. It is well-supported by specific details in the source: 'The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts' and 'The framework’s modular architecture... supports the integration of custom services... allowing DTs to evolve with changing operational requirements.'

The characteristic is extracted with high fidelity to the source, detailing the Model-Driven Engineering approach and evolution strategies. It is grounded in the text: 'Our approach automates the extraction of domain semantics and the generation of runtime interfaces, thereby reducing manual effort' and 'It also features standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms.'

The extraction correctly identifies the real-time synchronization, interactive visualization for monitoring, and the role of Facility Managers and custom analytics. However, it loses points because the phrase 'confidence derived from validated real-time data streams' is not explicitly supported; the text mentions validating the *model* and providing 'enhanced decision-making capabilities', but does not explicitly link confidence to validated streams. Quotes: 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization', 'interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control', 'Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts with the twin'.

The characteristic is well-grounded, identifying the adherence to BIM standards and the use of standardized extension points for interoperability in heterogeneous environments. Quotes: 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'compatibility with existing, AECO-specific systems and standards such as BIM', 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms', 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems'.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
